text:
Dear Margaret,

I have been meaning to write this for some time, and I suppose that delay itself speaks to how much your guidance has meant to me. The things that matter most are always the hardest to put into words.

When we first worked together, I was capable but unsteady, someone who could execute a task but who second-guessed every judgment call and retreated into overwork whenever I felt uncertain. You saw that pattern before I did, and you named it without making me feel diminished. That is a rare gift in a mentor, the ability to hold up an honest mirror while somehow keeping the other person willing to look into it.

What I remember most is not any single piece of advice, but the quality of your attention. You asked questions that forced me to slow down and think, and you were never in a hurry to give me the answer when I was capable of finding it myself. You seemed to understand that real growth is not something one person hands to another. It is something a person does for themselves, with the right company alongside them.

I also want to thank you for taking me seriously at a time when I was not entirely sure I deserved it. That confidence was not unearned on your part, but it was given generously, and I grew into it.

I am better at my work, and more importantly, I am more comfortable in my own judgment, because of the time you invested in me. I hope you know that those hours were not small. They added up to something that stays with me in rooms where you are no longer present.

With deep gratitude and affection,

David
